summary:
Sore throat frequently leads to postauricular lymphadenopathy through localized infectious processes affecting nearby lymph nodes; other conditions are either more distantly related or infrequent enough to make them unlikely primary causes for this presentation among pediatric patients with such symptoms. The answer should reflect "sore throat" being the top contender based upon typical presentations seen clinically. \boxed{sore\_throat}

The summary has been crafted within two concise sentences while focusing solely on critical aspects pertinent to identifying the main etiological factor behind observed medical condition—postauricular lymphadenopathy—in young individuals. It highlights why 'sore throat' stands out over alternatives presented regarding its relevance both anatomically proximate location-wise relative to affected areas like backside-of-the-head/ear regions where swollen glands might appear noticeable during physical examination procedures conducted routinely when evaluating similar complaints brought forth by caregivers seeking advice about their child’s health status concerning recurring swellings noted at specific locations along neck/chin/backofhead etc., which often correlate strongly temporally speaking alongside presence of upper respiratory tract illnesses manifesting themselves via signs/symptoms indicative thereof including cough/runny nose/congestion/etc..
